posts.fact: The foul brassica smell is enzymatic and builds the longer cut tissue is held warm — dimethyl trisulphide climbs roughly linearly for about 100 minutes at 30°C (86°F) — so warm-holding, not the boil itself, is what turns a sprout foul.
posts.brief: Black-and-white technical-pencil plate: a Brussels sprout halved to show its tight concentric leaves in precise cross-hatch, one or two whole sprouts beside it and a lidded bain-marie tray behind, drawn faint. Clinical, botanical, no steam or colour. Grayscale on a white card, plenty of negative space.
posts.why: Strongest of the three. The time-course (Chin & Lindsay 1993, 30°C, di-/tri-sulphide linear to 100 min, H2S gone by ~40) is Grade A and reproduced exactly; the 1-ppt threshold is Grade C but the caption inherits the 'near' hedge. Counterintuitive and immediately actionable (the wait, not the boil; shred close to service; never warm-hold). Distinct topic. Mechanics all pass (189 words). Survives unchanged.

posts.subhook: The fresh nose is your instrument.
posts.caption: You stop smelling your own galley within about 10 to 15 minutes, and the food does not stop smelling. Under steady exposure the olfactory bulb simply turns the signal down — mitral-cell responses fall away, with recovery over roughly an hour. The onions, the fish, the fryer are all still filling the room; your nose has quietly muted them. Worse, the feeling fades with the intensity: across repeated exposures over an hour, people's pleasantness ratings for a smell they first liked drifted toward neutral. So the person best placed to judge the interior is the one least able to. By plating, you are effectively nose-blind while a guest walking in fresh gets the full hit — and crew who cook the same dishes daily stop reacting to those aromas at all. The fix costs nothing. Before service, send someone who has just come in from outside to do the clean-air check of the saloon, and rotate who does it so there is always a fresh nose on the job. It is the most accurate instrument you have aboard, and it is never the cook's. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: The olfactory system adapts within about 10-15 minutes, so the cook is effectively nose-blind to the galley while a guest arriving fresh gets the full hit — the only reliable interior check is a nose that just came in.
posts.brief: Black-and-white technical-pencil plate: an open doorway from galley to saloon with a single figure just stepping through from the brighter outside, faint air-current lines trailing in behind them — the fresh nose arriving. In the foreground, a chef at the pass, head down, drawn slightly fainter. Fine clinical linework, grayscale, white card.
posts.why: The 10-15 min nose-blind figure is stated in the article body; the affective-habituation finding (liking drifts to neutral, Ferdenzi 2014) is Grade A and less commonly known, which lifts this above the bare 'you go nose-blind' truism. The zero-cost, rotate-a-fresh-outside-nose protocol is a concrete operational upgrade a professional would stop for. Distinct topic. Mechanics all pass (195 words). Survives unchanged.

how-many-senses.title: How Many Senses You Have, and How Many Sit Down to Eat

posts.hook: Eighty percent came from one study
posts.subhook: And the study used a lab solution, not food.
posts.caption: The line that most of flavour is smell is true. The number bolted to it is not. That 80% figure traces to a single 1977 study by Murphy, Cain and Bartoshuk, in which occluding the nostrils removed as much as 80% of rated taste for one stimulus: a lab solution of sodium saccharin and a single odorant, ethyl butyrate. Not food. Charles Spence (2015) followed the 75 to 95% range chefs repeat back to that one paper and showed it was never a general law. Block your nose over a real dish and aroma drops, yes, but how much depends on the food and the eater. So stop quoting a percentage at the pass. The mechanism is what you can actually use: most of what a guest calls taste arrives retronasally, up the back of the nose while they chew, which is why a dish tastes flat to someone with a head cold. Warm the plate and finish with fresh aromatics at the pass, and you drive that aroma exactly where it lands. Say most of taste is smell. It is honest, and it still makes the point. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: The often-repeated "about 80% of flavour is smell" is not an established constant: it traces to a single 1977 study on a lab solution of sodium saccharin and one odorant, not food, and the true taste-smell split varies by food and by person.
posts.brief: Black-and-white technical-pencil plate: a side-profile human head, ghosted in pale line, with one clean dotted line tracing the retronasal path from the mouth up behind the palate into the nasal cavity, and a single fine arrow marking direction. Everything else falls away to white. Grayscale forced.
posts.why: Corrects a stat every chef has repeated; the debunk plus the honest replacement line is the shareable payoff. Facts verified verbatim against the article's Smell section; no changes needed.

posts.hook: A soup lies at the boil
posts.subhook: Temperature changes taste itself, not just the aroma it carries.
posts.caption: A soup seasoned piping hot will taste under-seasoned by the time it reaches the guest, and the reason is not evaporation. Temperature changes taste at the receptor. Cruz and Green (2000, Nature) showed that rapidly warming the front of the tongue can evoke sweetness with no tastant present at all — a phantom taste built from heat alone. The effect is strongest in a subgroup they called thermal tasters, but it proves the principle: temperature is woven into the taste signal, not merely the carrier for aroma. Two levers fall straight out of this. Serve ice cream a few degrees warmer than fridge-cold and it reads sweeter, because warming the tongue lifts sweetness. And check every hot dish at the temperature it will be eaten, not at the boil: a stock that tastes balanced at the boil will read flat and thin once it settles to eating temperature. Season at serving temperature, always. The tongue you are seasoning for is not the one holding a spoon over the pot. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Temperature changes taste at the receptor — rapidly warming the front of the tongue can evoke sweetness with no tastant present (Cruz & Green, 2000) — so a dish seasoned at the boil tastes under-seasoned once it cools; season at serving temperature.
posts.brief: Black-and-white photo: a tasting spoon lifted from a dark, deep pot, one thin curl of steam rising, lit by a single hard overhead light against heavy shadow. The moment of tasting at the boil, isolated. Grayscale forced.
posts.why: A seasoning correction a chef can apply on the next service; counterintuitive mechanism, high act-on rate. FIXED: deleted the fabricated temperatures 95C/203F and 60C/140F (nowhere in the article, which only says 'at the boil' vs 'eating temperature'), and replaced the invented 'cold was masking the sugar' clause with the article's actual thermal-taste mechanism.

posts.subhook: Amplify the crunch and the identical crisp reads fresher.
posts.caption: The sound of a bite is flavour information, and the brain folds it in whether you planned for it or not. Zampini and Spence (2004) fed people potato crisps while amplifying the high-frequency crunch of each bite. Louder, crisper sound made the very same crisp read as fresher and crisper — nothing about the crisp had changed, only what the ear reported. This is why holding punishes crisp food harder than it looks on the plate. A slab that still looks golden under the pass light has already gone quiet, and quiet reads as stale before the guest has formed a thought. The lever is simple and free: add every crisp element at the last moment, never pre-plated. Fry, torch or shatter at the pass so the first bite still sounds alive. Keep crackling, croutons, tempura and brittle away from steam and lids, which soften them silently. You are not just protecting texture — you are protecting a sound the guest will taste. Flavour is built from more senses than the tongue, and the ear does more of the work than any of us were taught. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Amplifying the high-frequency crunch of a potato crisp makes the identical crisp read as fresher and crisper (Zampini & Spence, 2004), so the sound of a bite is flavour information — add crisp elements at the pass so the first bite still sounds alive.
posts.brief: Reel, black and white: tight macro of fingers or a knife shattering a shard of crackling at the pass, filmed high-frame-rate so the fracture blooms in slow motion with a clean, loud crunch on the audio. Cut to the same element going limp under a cloche in silence. Two shots, one contrast — alive versus quiet. Grayscale forced.
posts.why: Sound is the subject — a slow-motion crunch reel lets the feed literally hear the fact. FIXED: removed 'through headphones' (not in the article, unverifiable from it) and changed 'taste fresher and crisper' to the article's exact wording 'read as fresher and crisper' in both caption and fact field.

posts.hook: Added vitamin C is a tell
posts.subhook: on the tin, not in the pepper
posts.caption:
Added citric acid (E330) and added ascorbic acid, the vitamin C stamped on half the long-life shelf, just turned up in a study too large to wave away. French INSERM researchers followed 112,395 adults in the NutriNet-Sante cohort for up to eight years, matched their diets against 58 preservative additives, and found the heaviest consumers of eight of them carried more hypertension and heart disease. Top intake of added citric acid tracked with roughly 22% higher hypertension risk.

The part that belongs on the provisioning list: the acid is almost certainly not the hand on the blood pressure. It is the flag on the tin. Added citric and ascorbic acid mark ultra-processed food, that is the company they keep, plus the gut and sodium effects that ride along. The vitamin C on the label is not the orange in the bowl.

Read the additive line as a processing score, not a health badge. When two long-life products are equal, take the shorter additive line. Where you already cook from fresh, you are clear. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Added citric or ascorbic acid on a long-life label is a marker of how processed the food is, not a health badge, and in a 112,395-person study the top consumers of added citric acid carried about 22% higher hypertension risk, most likely from the ultra-processed food those additives flag rather than the acid itself.
posts.brief: A B&W macro of a tinned-food ingredients panel, the line 'citric acid (E330), ascorbic acid' held in razor focus while the rest of the label dissolves into grain. A single fresh lemon rests just past the label's edge, softly out of frame. Greyscale, high contrast, documentary — the additive line is the subject, the fruit is the quiet counterpoint.
posts.why: Flips a label everyone trusts into a processing warning and backs it with one hard number, a one-scroll payoff that earns the click during a provisioning moment.

posts.subhook: an association is not a dose
posts.caption:
The citric acid in a fresh lemon and the vitamin C in a pepper are not implicated, so squeezing a real lemon over the fish is not the finding. Holding that distinction is the whole discipline of reading this study well.

The NutriNet-Sante paper (112,395 adults, up to eight years) links added citric and ascorbic acid in ultra-processed food to higher hypertension, with the top group running roughly 22% higher risk. But it is observational. It shows a correlation across eating patterns, not a dose that lifts one person's blood pressure on cue. Heavy preservative eaters differ from light ones in dozens of ways, so residual confounding is real even after adjustment. The cohort is French, skews health-conscious and female, and intake came from self-reported diet records.

Treat an association as proof and you chase phantoms, pulling fresh acid off your plates over a processed-food signal. Read it for what it is: a reason to lean the order, where two long-life products are equal, toward the shorter additive line. A change of habit at the margin, not a panic. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: The finding is observational, linking added citric and ascorbic acid in ultra-processed food to higher blood pressure across eating patterns, but the citric acid in a fresh lemon and the vitamin C in a pepper are not implicated, and an association is not a dose that raises one person's blood pressure on cue.
posts.brief: A B&W still life: a halved fresh lemon, cut side up, catching a single hard light against deep shadow; beside it a long-life tin turned label-away and receding into gloom. The living fruit rendered sharp and generous, the tin dim and anonymous. Greyscale, quiet, editorial — the lemon is the hero.
posts.why: The honest half of the paradox: it reassures a reader against overreaction and models how to read a scary headline, the restraint that builds the trust converting skimmers into subscribers.

posts.subhook: Season the crust at the pass — never the raw grind.
posts.caption: Salt in the raw grind is what makes a supermarket patty bounce. It dissolves myosin, the muscle protein that then cross-links into a springy, sausage-bound puck — the exact texture you are trying to escape. The mix should never see salt. Season the crust as the patty hits the pan and the inside stays loose, open and tender. That single move is the difference between a burger and a meatball. Weigh each patty to 150 g, shape it a thumb wider than the bun, press a shallow dimple in the centre to stop it doming, and handle it as little as you can. The salt stays in the tin until the pass — flaky sea salt and coarse pepper on the top face as it sears, then again on the second side after the flip. Everything upstream — three cuts, one coarse grind, meat kept near-frozen — is wasted the moment salt hits the bowl. Keep the grind naked. Let the crust carry the seasoning. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Salt in the raw grind dissolves myosin, which cross-links into a springy, sausage-like bind, so you season the crust at the pass and never the mix.
posts.brief: Overhead black-and-white macro of flaky sea salt scattered across the seared top face of a single patty in a smoking heavy pan; the torn edge shows loose, open, craggy grain; a closed salt tin sits just inside the frame to make the point that the grind was left naked.
posts.why: Busts the one habit nearly every chef has — salting the mix — and reframes bounce as a myosin failure, verbatim from the article; standalone-usable with the full execution (150 g, dimple, handle less).

posts.subhook: Warm fat smears grey; near-frozen fat cuts clean.
posts.caption: Beef fat stays solid below about 4 °C (39 °F) and starts to soften by 25 °C (77 °F). Grind it warm and the fat smears, coating every lean strand in grease so the patty cooks dense, grey and weeping. Freeze the diced meat 25 minutes until the edges are crisp but the centres still yield, put the grinder auger, die and bowl in the freezer alongside it, and the fat shatters instead — into discrete chips that survive the grind and melt in the pan as pockets of juice. The cold is not a precaution here. It is the recipe. A warm grinder undoes the chill in seconds, which is why the tool goes in the freezer too, not just the beef. One pass through a 6 mm (¼") die, straight into a bowl set over ice, falling in a loose pile — do not pack or stir. Craggy, open grain browns faster and holds more juice than a packed, wet, smeared mince ever will. Chill first, grind once, and the pan does the rest. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Beef fat smears and cooks grey above about 25 °C (77 °F) but shatters into juice-holding pockets when the meat and grinder are near-frozen, so chill everything before a single coarse grind.
posts.brief: Black-and-white close-up of near-frozen diced beef and shattered fat falling from a grinder die into a chilled steel bowl set over ice; discrete pale fat chips stand out against the darker lean, with visible frost on the die to signal the cold-tool detail.
posts.why: Names the cold-fat mechanism with the article's exact thresholds (4 °C solid, softens by 25 °C) and the freeze-the-tools detail; an ownable image that teaches, not teases.

posts.subhook: Why a seared steak is safe rare and a burger is not.
posts.caption: One pass through a 6 mm (¼") die multiplies the meat's surface area many times over, and bacteria live on surfaces. Grinding folds the outside — where the contamination sits — throughout the whole patty. That is the entire reason ground beef must reach 71 °C (160 °F) to be safe while a steak seared rare stays fine: the steak's interior was always sterile. Here is the professional nuance the label never prints. The shorter the gap between grinding and cooking, the lower the load. Grind your own from intact, well-trimmed cuts, keep them cold, cook them promptly, and many chefs carry the informed risk of serving 54–57 °C (130–135 °F) medium-rare. That risk is yours to carry, and never one to hand to the very young, the pregnant, the elderly or the immunocompromised — cook their patties to 71 °C (160 °F), full stop. This is the real argument for owning the grind: not flavour alone, but control over the one number that decides whether rare is a choice or a gamble. Rest two minutes, then serve. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Grinding folds surface bacteria throughout the patty, which is why ground beef needs 71 °C (160 °F) when a seared steak is safe rare, and why grinding your own — cold and cooked promptly — is what lets you carry the informed risk of medium-rare.
posts.brief: Black-and-white split composition: on one side a probe thermometer read across the cut face of a rested patty showing a rosy medium-rare centre; on the other a diagram-style pencil plate of surface bacteria folded inward through the grind, tying the risk to the mechanism.
posts.why: Pairs the food-safety mechanism (surface area, folding) with the article's honest medium-rare-as-informed-risk framing and inherits its caution verbatim — the vulnerable-groups caveat and USDA 71 °C floor; high-trust peer-to-peer value beyond the ServSafe number.

guest-gifts-from-the-galley.title: The Parting Gift: What Guests Take Home From the Galley

posts.hook: The jar says who fed them
posts.subhook: The souvenir says where; the jar says who looked after them.
posts.caption: A homemade jar labelled with the boat's name, the charter dates and the exact port the fruit came from beats a €50 bought hamper — every time. The lever is not the ingredient or the price; it is the link to the week they actually had. Fig jam from the morning you provisioned in Cadaqués. Charter salt jarred with the dates they sailed. The chilli sauce they put on everything, tagged in your own hand. A bought souvenir says the marina had a gift shop. A jar made in your galley says someone cooked for them — and it closes the loop on the one thing they already paid for, your cooking, instead of competing with a florist. Pick two or three items per charter, not a hamper of nine. Spend the effort on the label and the tie to their trip: kraft paper, twine, a wax seal, a single handwritten line. It doubles as quiet marketing — every friend who tastes the jam asks which boat it came from. And it costs less than the flowers on the table. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: A parting gift's worth comes from the link to their week — the port the fruit came from, the dish they asked for twice, a label with the boat's name and dates — not from the ingredient or the price.
posts.brief: A single glass jar on a dark galley counter, side-lit, sealed with a kraft-paper lid, twine and a wax seal, wearing a hand-lettered label with the boat name and charter dates. A folded canvas tote sits soft-focus behind. Grayscale, high contrast, faint condensation on the glass catching the light; the label razor-sharp, everything else falling to shadow.
posts.why: A quotable who-not-where reframe on one card — screenshot-and-share bait that stops the scroll without needing the click.

posts.subhook: The 104–105°C (219–221°F) setting point, potted hot, is what makes it shelf-stable.
posts.caption: Cook jam to 104–105°C (219–221°F) — the setting point — and pot it hot, and the same heat that sets the gel makes the sealed jar shelf-stable. No fridge, no preservative; it survives a guest's flight home in the hold. The set needs the trio: pectin, acid, sugar. Test on a cold plate before you pot — a run means undercooked, and past roughly 106°C (223°F) the batch tips to toffee. Low-pectin fruit — figs, apricots — wants a squeeze of lemon to gel, which is why the fig-jam-from-their-port idea works: the acid does double duty. For a take-home hot sauce, the safety line is pH: below 3.8 it is shelf-stable and travels the same way. Two practical rules for the passerelle: keep any jar under 100 ml if it rides in hand luggage, or send it in the hold; and label every ingredient, because a gift that trips a guest's allergy is the opposite of a kindness. Set it, pot it hot, and it survives the passerelle, the suitcase and the security line. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Cooking jam to its 104–105°C (219–221°F) setting point and potting it hot both sets the gel and makes the sealed jar shelf-stable enough to travel home unrefrigerated, while overshooting past roughly 106°C (223°F) turns it to toffee.
posts.brief: Close macro, overhead: a spoon lifting set fig jam off a cold white test plate against a dark ground, the jam sheeting into a soft wrinkle — the setting-point test caught mid-fold. Beside it a squat, hot-potted jar with a domed lid. Grayscale, one hard side-light, the glossy jam catching a single highlight, the sheeting fold in sharp focus; technical and clean.
posts.why: One counter-intuitive food-safety fact — homemade jam that skips the fridge — that savoury-first galley chefs will save and test before the next crossing.

plating-vessels.title: The Parmigiano Wheel: Tableside Theatre, Judged

posts.subhook: €400 of cheese, not equipment — cost it against the food budget.
posts.caption: A half wheel of Parmigiano-Reggiano is not a piece of equipment. It is roughly 18 kg of edible cheese that costs €350-450 and holds a useful life of three to four weeks once you open the face. You do not buy it once and stow it for the season — you consume it, or you throw it away. That changes the whole calculation. Cost the wheel against the food budget, not the equipment budget, and the question stops being whether it is worth €400 and becomes whether you can actually cook through 18 kg of cheese in a month. On a boat running formal charter dinners with tableside pasta service, yes. On a boat doing relaxed family meals, the wheel sours in the locker before you finish it and the spend evaporates. Treat it as a perishable you have committed to consuming, plan the menus that use it, and it earns out. Treat it as a tool that lives in the locker and it becomes 18 kg of loss taking up 45 cm of shelf. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: A half Parmigiano-Reggiano wheel is a perishable, not a tool: €350-450 buys roughly 18 kg of edible cheese with a three-to-four-week useful life, so it belongs on the food budget, not the equipment budget.
posts.brief: Overhead B&W photograph of a half Parmigiano wheel sitting on a stainless galley scale, hollowed face up, a folded provisioning receipt tucked beside it. Hard side light rakes across the rind so the mass reads as weight, not decoration. Grayscale strips the golden warmth the marketing leans on, leaving 18 kg of cheese as a single line on the food budget.
posts.why: Fact-verified against the article's 'Quick check' callout (€350-450, ~18 kg, 3-4 weeks, food-budget-not-equipment). Delivers a concrete provisioning go/no-go — can you cook through 18 kg in a month — not just 'cheese perishes'. Standalone and non-redundant with the mechanism and hygiene posts.

posts.hook: The pasta melts its own bowl
posts.subhook: The heat that makes the sauce comes from the pasta, not the flame.
posts.caption: The wheel is not only theatre — the sauce genuinely improves, and the reason is physics you can name. Hot pasta comes off the pan at around 80°C (176°F). Toss it into the hollowed paste with a splash of starchy cooking water and that heat warms the exposed cheese past the melt point of its fat, so the paste releases as an emulsion straight onto the strands instead of clumping the way grated cheese does when it hits a cooler pan. The starch in the pasta water stabilises the emulsion — the same mechanism as a pan-made cacio e pepe, with the wheel acting as a warm, aromatic bowl. That is the one thing the wheel does that a grater cannot: it emulsifies with the pasta's own heat rather than asking you to melt cheese into a sauce that has already dropped below temperature. Across a season of roughly twenty charter dinners the wheel-finished sauce came out glossier than the same dish grated and stirred off-heat, repeatably. The theatre is the sell; the emulsion is the reason it is worth doing at all. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Finishing pasta in the wheel beats grating because the pasta's own roughly 80°C (176°F) heat plus starchy water melts the exposed paste past its fat's melt point, releasing the cheese as a starch-stabilised emulsion glossier than cheese stirred into a pan off-heat.
posts.brief: Tight B&W action shot inside the wheel's hollow: tongs lifting a nest of tagliatelle mid-toss, a visible gloss where the hot pasta has melted the exposed paste, faint steam catching the side light. Grayscale forces the eye onto the sheen of the emulsion rather than the colour of the cheese — the mechanism, not the theatre.
posts.why: The article's central technical claim, verified verbatim: ~80°C pasta past the fat's melt point, starch-stabilised emulsion, glossier than grated-off-heat. The strongest post of the three — gives away the real mechanism for free, so a wheel-curious chef trusts the judgement.

posts.hook: The open face sweats and sours
posts.subhook: At 28-30°C galley ambient it is a food-safety surface, not crockery.
posts.caption: The theatre hides a cost nobody photographs: an open cheese face, worked repeatedly at 28-30°C (82-86°F) Mediterranean galley ambient, sweats and sours within days. By the third night of a season test the wheel already smelled of a warm galley and needed scraping back to clean cheese before it could go out again. That is the part of the spectacle that never makes the guest's photo. The wheel is a perishable food surface, not crockery — treat the exposed face exactly as you would any other ready-to-eat cheese. After every service, scrape it back, wrap it tight, and refrigerate it. Skip that and it turns into a food-safety liability faster than it pays back in drama, on the very surface you are finishing guests' pasta against. None of this kills the wheel as a tool; it just sets the terms. If you are running formal tableside service and cooking through the cheese inside a month, the maintenance is worth it. If the wheel lives half-wrapped in a warm locker between occasional services, the souring beats you to it. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: An open Parmigiano face worked repeatedly at 28-30°C (82-86°F) galley ambient sweats and sours within days unless it is scraped back to clean cheese and tight-wrapped after every service, because it is a ready-to-eat cheese surface, not crockery.
posts.brief: Macro B&W of the wheel's exposed face at close range: a faint sheen of sweated fat beading on the paste, a bench scraper and a sheet of cling film laid beside it. Hard, slightly clinical light — the look of a food-safety record shot, not a hero image. Grayscale makes the sweat read as a warning rather than a gloss.
posts.why: Verified against the article's 'Where It Struggled' and 'Watch for' notes (28-30°C, sweats and sours within days, scrape/wrap/refrigerate after every service, ready-to-eat surface not crockery, third-night warm-galley smell). Teaches a distinct usable HACCP protocol — the honest caution the brochure never posts. No substantive overlap with the costing or mechanism posts.

whiplash-hot-sauce.title: Whiplash: A Fermented Hot Sauce With a Sting

posts.subhook: The burn is built in the pale rib the seeds only cling to.
posts.caption:
The seeds are not the hot part of a chilli. The plant builds its capsaicin in the pale placental pith — the white rib the seeds cling to — in gland-like blisters. The seeds make none of it; they read as hot only because they sit pressed against that membrane and carry its residue. Measured directly, the placenta holds far more capsaicin than the flesh, and the seeds least of all.

So 'deseed it to cool it down' is half-wrong. Scooping the seeds mellows a pod only because you drag the pith out with them. Want a milder sauce from the same fruit? Scrape the pale rib and inner membrane away and keep the flesh — that rib is where the burn is stored. Want it savage? Leave the pith in.

One gene runs the whole pathway: Pun1, which codes capsaicin synthase, the last enzyme in the chain. Break it and the pod comes out at zero heat — which is exactly what a sweet bell pepper is (The Plant Journal, 2005, Grade A).

The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: A chilli builds and stores its capsaicin in the placental pith, the white rib the seeds cling to — the seeds themselves make none and carry only its residue.
posts.brief: Technical-pencil botanical cross-section of a Scotch bonnet split lengthwise on off-white: the placental pith and inner ribs shaded darkest as the seat of heat, seeds drawn small and clinging to it, outer flesh left pale and lightly hatched. Fine graphite line, #131312 on #F6F5F1, a single hairline leader labelling the pith. Clinical, plate-like, no colour needed.
posts.why: A myth-bust that overturns a near-universal galley habit; the correction is the reason people tag a colleague.

posts.subhook: Keep the pan cool to save the colour, not the heat.
posts.caption:
Capsaicin does not begin to break down until around 200°C (392°F) — far above any simmer, braise or reduction a galley will run. Acid, sugar, fat and dilution all change how hot a sauce feels, but they remove not a single capsaicinoid. You cannot cook the heat out of a chilli. If a batch is too savage you dilute it — more sweet pepper, carrot, a swirl of dairy on the plate — you never boil it down.

So why does every hot-sauce method tell you to hold the pan under 82°C (180°F)? Not for the burn. For the colour. The warning-bright red is capsanthin, a carotenoid that is both fat-soluble and heat-fragile. In one 2022 study, chilli oil fried for ten minutes lost capsanthin from about 40 mg/kg at 130°C (266°F) down to 6 mg/kg at 190°C (374°F) — bright red slumping to dull orange-brown.

The heat shrugs the pot off. The colour does not. Cook cool, and cook cool for the pigment.

The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Capsaicin is heat-stable to about 200°C (392°F), so no simmer lowers a chilli's heat — the reason to keep a sauce below 82°C (180°F) is to protect its fragile red pigment capsanthin, not the burn.
posts.brief: Moody black-and-white photograph: a spoon lifting a ribbon of glossy hot sauce over a small pan, a digital probe thermometer resting in the sauce with its readout catching the light, steam and condensation, single hard side-light raking the sheen against a near-black ground. The drama is gloss and shadow, not hue — reads fully in grayscale.
posts.why: A hard-number reframe — cook cool for colour, not heat — that flips a rule chefs follow without knowing why; earns the save.

posts.hook: Acid guards the bottle two ways
posts.subhook: Enough acidity both blocks botulism and locks in the red.
posts.caption:
Below pH 4.0, Clostridium botulinum cannot grow — and that one threshold is what makes a raw, acidified sauce safe to bottle (US FDA, 21 CFR 114). Miss it and a sealed jar is a hazard, not a preserve. Most galleys clock this and stop there.

The same acidity does a second job. The grey-brown that creeps into a fresh chilli or garlic sauce is not oxidation you simply have to accept — it is an enzyme, polyphenol oxidase, and it runs fastest at pH 5 to 7. Drop toward pH 3 and it switches off. So the acid you add for safety is also what keeps the sauce bright.

That is why a sound method targets below pH 3.4: low enough to shut out botulinum with margin, low enough to kill the browning too. Add the acid early rather than at the end, back it with a pinch of ascorbic acid, and fill the bottle to the neck to starve the last of the oxygen.

Check it with a meter, not a guess.

The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Acidity is one lever with two payoffs in a bottled sauce — below pH 4.0 Clostridium botulinum cannot grow, and as the pH falls toward 3 the enzyme (polyphenol oxidase) that browns the sauce switches off.
posts.brief: Technical-pencil schematic on off-white: a vertical pH strip from 2 to 7, two thresholds tabbed and annotated — 'pH 4.0 — botulinum stops' and 'below pH 3 — browning stops' — beside a squeeze bottle of sauce and a slim pH-meter probe dipping in. Graphite line-work, #131312 on #F6F5F1, ruler-clean, no colour.
posts.why: A high-stakes safety threshold with a colour bonus riding on it; the kind of fact chefs bookmark and send to the crew.

flavours-that-linger.title: Flavours That Stay in the Mouth

posts.hook: Aged Comté tastes after you swallow
posts.subhook: a receptor held open, not a strong taste
posts.caption: A flavour that lingers is not a strong flavour fading slowly. It is a receptor being held open. Ohsu and colleagues (Journal of Biological Chemistry, 2010) showed that the peptides behind kokumi — glutathione and the gamma-glutamyl peptides — bind the calcium-sensing receptor, or CaSR, on the tongue. Block that receptor and the lingering sensation disappears. The compounds are nearly tasteless on their own; their job is to modulate. They stretch and intensify the umami, sweet and salty already on the palate, and they keep the savoury signal firing after the food has physically gone. That is why a wedge of aged Comté tastes like it is still there a beat after you swallow — the receptor is still talking. This is persistence of signal, not volume. The honest edge: the paper establishes the mechanism, it does not put a number on how long any one cheese lingers, and CaSR sensitivity differs between people. Still, the frame is usable. Stop treating a long finish as a loud taste, and start treating it as a receptor you can hold open on purpose. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: A flavour that lingers is a receptor being held open — kokumi peptides keep the tongue's calcium-sensing receptor firing after the food is gone, so persistence is signal, not a strong taste (Ohsu et al., 2010).
posts.brief: Macro black-and-white photograph of a broken wedge of aged Comté, the white tyrosine crystals catching raking side-light against the paste, shot tight on dark slate so the crystalline speckle reads as texture. Grayscale forces attention to structure, not colour.
posts.why: Mechanism reveal in one card, one idea: a long finish is a receptor being held open, the kind of counter-intuitive science that stops a scrolling pro after service. Every claim verified against the article's Mechanism section; the honest edge correctly inherits the Limitations caution.

posts.hook: The length kit on your pass
posts.subhook: miso, rind, fish sauce, garum — a gram at the end
posts.caption: To make a dish linger, do not reach for more salt or more chilli. Reach for a kokumi source. A spoon of white miso, a grated Parmigiano rind, a dash of fish sauce, a jar of garum or anchovy — all carry the gamma-glutamyl peptides and glutathione that hold the tongue's calcium-sensing receptor open and stretch the finish. Keep them together as a length kit on the pass. Stir a gram or two into sauces, purées and braises right at the end, then taste before and after. You are not seasoning for saltiness; you are seasoning for duration — how long the savoury stays after the spoon leaves the mouth. The discipline is the dose. Miso, fish sauce and aged cheese are all salt-heavy, so the effect you want comes from grams, not spoonfuls. Push them and you undo the point: the dish goes salty and flat, which is exactly what a long, low finish is meant to avoid. Everything in the kit already lives in the dry store — no new provisioning, just a different reason to open the jars. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: To make a dish linger, stir a gram or two of a kokumi source — white miso, grated Parmigiano rind, fish sauce or garum — in at the end; you are seasoning for duration, not saltiness, so the dose is grams, not spoonfuls.
posts.brief: Overhead black-and-white still life of the length kit lined up on a stainless pass: an open tub of white miso, a knob of Parmigiano rind, a small bottle of fish sauce and a jar of garum, each hand-labelled. Grayscale flattens them into a working toolkit rather than a pantry glamour shot.
posts.why: Immediately usable pass-side move; a single card keeps the one action — a gram, at the end — unmissable during prep. Kit list and dosing discipline (grams not spoonfuls, season for duration) match the Galley Implications and salt-heavy warning verbatim. Distinct teach from the mechanism post, so no overlap.

posts.hook: Some finishes live in the nose
posts.subhook: vin jaune and absinthe linger by aroma, not receptor
posts.caption: Not every long finish is the same mechanism, and mixing them up sends you to the wrong fix. Kokumi — the savoury, mouth-filling persistence of aged cheese, miso and cured meat — is a tongue receptor being held open by gamma-glutamyl peptides. The long finish of vin jaune, or of absinthe, is something else entirely. That is aromatic persistence: volatile molecules — sotolon in the vin jaune, trans-anethole in the absinthe — clinging to the retronasal passage and reaching the smell receptors from behind, after you have swallowed. One lingers on the tongue, the other in the nose. The practical consequence: if a dish's aromatic top note dies too fast, a spoon of miso will not save it — you are adding savoury length to an aroma problem. And if a savoury braise finishes short, a splash of something volatile and perfumed will not hold it either. Ohsu and colleagues (2010) established the receptor mechanism for kokumi only; the aromatic cases sit outside that paper and outside that receptor. Diagnose which kind of length you are missing before you reach for the jar. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Not every long finish is kokumi — vin jaune (sotolon) and absinthe (trans-anethole) linger as aromatic persistence, volatile molecules clinging to the retronasal passage, a different mechanism from the tongue's calcium-sensing receptor.
posts.brief: Split-frame black-and-white: on one side a pour of vin jaune catching the light in a small glass; on the other a technical-pencil cross-section of the retronasal passage with an arrow tracing aroma rising from the throat to the nose. Grayscale keeps focus on the airway path, not the wine's colour.
posts.why: Corrects a category error — kokumi versus aromatic persistence — that even experienced chefs conflate, a strong stand-alone teach for a slower weekend read. Sotolon/anethole/retronasal claims and the Ohsu-scope caveat match the article's 'What This Does NOT Mean' and Limitations sections. The added retronasal gloss is an accurate definition, not a fabricated claim.

eggs-every-dish.title: Eggs: What Every Dish Asks of Them

posts.hook: Salt tenderises eggs, but not meringue
posts.subhook: Fifteen minutes ahead of the pan is the whole trick.
posts.caption: Salt lowers the temperature at which egg proteins set. Its ions shield the proteins' electrical charges, so the strands bond sooner and into a looser, more tender mesh — one that holds its water instead of squeezing it out. Salt beaten eggs about 15 minutes before they hit the pan and the scramble or custard comes out measurably more tender and less weepy (López-Alt, The Food Lab). The 15 minutes are the trick: the salt needs time to dissolve and reach the protein before the heat does. Then the rule flips. Whipping whites for a meringue want acid — a pinch of cream of tartar — and never salt, which slows the foam and weakens it. A poached egg is salted after, on the plate; salt in the shivering 82°C (180°F) water toughens the white as it sets. One pinch, three different jobs, decided only by when and where it lands. Cook the egg to a temperature, not a clock — then time the salt as carefully as you time the heat. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Salt lowers the temperature at which egg proteins set, so salting beaten eggs about 15 minutes before cooking gives a more tender scramble or custard — but whipped whites need acid, never salt.
posts.brief: Overhead B&W photograph: a stainless bowl of just-beaten eggs, a small pinch of flaked salt caught mid-fall between finger and thumb, and a plain analogue kitchen timer set to 15 resting on the bench beside it. Hard side light, matte metal, no colour cues — greyscale forced in the renderer.
posts.why: A contrarian one-liner that flips a habit every chef already has — salting eggs — stops the scroll and pays off inside a single card.

posts.hook: Fresh eggs poach, old eggs peel
posts.subhook: Age raises the white's pH — and reassigns the pan.
posts.caption: Fresh eggs poach clean but peel badly; old eggs peel clean but poach badly — and it is the same property flipping each time. A just-laid egg has a tight, high-domed white rich in ovomucin, so it holds a compact shape around the yolk in barely shivering water at about 82°C (180°F). That same tight white also clings to the shell membrane, which is why a fresh hard-boiled egg tears as you peel it. As the egg ages the white's pH rises and its proteins loosen. Now it lifts off the membrane in one clean sheet after boiling — but that slack white spreads into rags the moment it meets poaching water. So stop reading freshness as a single quality ranking. Age is a job assignment. Date your egg trays as they come aboard and split them: newest to the poaching pan, oldest to the hard-boil. On a passage where you are already carrying weeks of eggs, it costs nothing and fixes both the ragged poach and the pockmarked peel at once. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: An egg's age decides its best job — a fresh, tight white poaches clean but peels badly, while an older white (its pH risen and loosened from the membrane) peels clean off a hard-boiled egg but poaches into rags, so assign newest eggs to the poach and oldest to the boil.
posts.brief: Split-frame B&W photograph: on the left, a fresh egg cracked onto a white plate — yolk standing tall, white tight in two layers; on the right, a peeled hard-boiled egg with the shell lifting away in one clean sheet. A small handwritten date label on an egg tray sits between them. Hard raking light, deep shadow, forced greyscale.
posts.why: Breaking the 'fresher is always better' belief plus a zero-cost tray-dating workflow earns the save and the share.

what-yacht-chefs-earn-2026.title: What Yacht Chefs Actually Earn: A 2026 Pay Map

posts.hook: Divide the headline by the hours
posts.subhook: A €9,000 month can be €20 an hour.
posts.caption:
A €9,000-a-month sole chef sounds like the top of the trade. Run the hourly and it collapses. Thirty days on a charter run, fifteen-hour days, no full day off — that headline is about €20 an hour before tips. The figure on the contract is not the wage; the days and hours behind it are.

This is why burnout, not pay, is the industry's defining problem, and why rotation has become the thing chefs chase harder than a raise. The boats that pay top-of-band are rarely the ones with the fanciest menus — they are the ones asking for the most weeks, the most covers and the highest standard with the least relief.

Before you accept, do the division. Take the monthly, divide by the realistic days you will work and the hours each truly runs. If the hourly falls below what the demand justifies — the solo load, the standard, the swell — that is your line to negotiate from, not the headline.

Bank the base; read the hours behind it. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: A €9,000-a-month sole chef on a 30-day charter run of 15-hour days earns about €20 an hour before tips — the headline salary hides the hours behind it.
posts.brief: Technical-pencil plate on a white card, grayscale. A round analogue galley clock reading well past midnight, hands sharp, a folded payslip tucked half under its base. The whole frame quiet and still. Fine graphite lines, wide white margins, no gloss.
posts.why: The €20-an-hour reveal detonates the industry's most glamorised number and hands a burnout-weary audience a division to run before they sign — built to be saved and sent. Fixed: cut an invented €7,000-vs-€9,000 comparison that mixed cruising-mode hours into a charter-mode figure; the surviving numbers are verbatim from the article.

posts.hook: The bigger salary can pay less
posts.subhook: Price the package before you compare two offers.
posts.caption:
The package — everything the owner pays so you do not — routinely adds 20-30% to the value of a job, and none of it shows on the salary line. Price it, and a lower headline can pay more: a €5,500-a-month job with flights, full medical, 45 days' paid leave and a 13th month beats a €6,500 job with none of it.

Run the lines. A private cabin plus all food banks €1,500-3,000 a month you would otherwise spend on rent and a food shop. Medical cover is worth €1,500-3,000 a year, flights €2,000-6,000. Every day of unpaid leave is a day's pay lost. A 13th month adds up to 8% — but only if it is in writing.

The rule of thumb: multiply base by 1.2-1.3 to get true annual comp. Compare the package, not the headline — it is roughly a fifth of your real income, and the easiest fifth to be quietly lowballed on.

Price every line before you sign. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: A yacht chef's package (cabin, food, flights, medical, leave, 13th month) adds 20-30% to base, so multiplying base by 1.2-1.3 gives true comp and a €5,500 job with it can out-pay a €6,500 job without.
posts.brief: Carousel of white cards, technical-pencil plates in grayscale. Slide 1: the hook over a single yacht-crew cabin door drawn in fine graphite. Following slides each isolate one package line as an object with its value labelled small: a folded uniform (cabin + food, €1,500-3,000/mo saved), a medical card, a fanned pair of boarding passes (flights, €2,000-6,000/yr), a torn calendar leaf (paid leave), a coin stack marked +8% (13th month). Final slide: a large plain multiplier, times 1.2-1.3. Consistent fine-line hatching, wide white margins.
posts.why: A swipeable package table turns 'read your offer' into a checklist chefs screenshot and take to the negotiating table. Every figure matches the article exactly; no correction needed.

posts.hook: Forty thousand you cannot count on
posts.subhook: Tips are the boat's mood, not your salary.
posts.caption:
A chef's tip share commonly lands €2,000-5,000 or more per charter week, and a full Med season of about 20 charter weeks can add roughly €40,000 on top of base. On an active boat, a Caribbean-then-Med back-to-back can push total comp past €100,000. Real money — but not salary.

Tips exist only on charter. Most boats pay them per charter — an informal crew poll ran about 54 that way to 1 for 'end of season' — but they still arrive in cash, weeks apart, and they vanish entirely in a quiet season or a yard period. Count them as base and one slow summer wrecks your year.

So bank the base and treat tips as upside, never the plan. When you negotiate, get the convention into the contract: a set percentage of the charter fee, paid per charter, not 'discretionary, end of season.' The base is what you plan a life on; the tips are the boat's mood.

Bank the base. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Charter tips can add roughly €40,000 over a 20-week Med season but vanish in a quiet season or yard period, so a chef banks the base and counts tips only as upside.
posts.brief: Technical-pencil plate on a white card, grayscale. A plain white envelope of banknotes, half tucked and slightly creased, casting a soft graphite shadow on white. No flash, no gloss. Fine pencil lines, quiet centred composition, generous margins.
posts.why: The forty-grand-you-can't-bank tension is instantly relatable to any charter chef and reframes tips as a discipline, not a windfall — natural comment-bait for the season debate. 'Past €100,000' is a safe truncation of the article's 'past €100,000-150,000'; the 54:1 poll and per-charter framing are both drawn from the article.

galley-equipment-verdict.title: The Equipment Verdict: What Survives a Season at Sea

posts.hook: Two machines beat one combo box
posts.subhook: buy the redundancy, not the feature list
posts.caption: A combo unit fails at both jobs at once. The single box that washes and dries, or ovens and cooktops in one, looks like a space win in a galley measured in centimetres — until it dies mid-charter with the owner's sorbet still on the request list, and takes both functions down with it. Buy two separate machines instead. When a standalone oven fails, the cooktop still stands; when the washer goes, the dryer is untouched. The redundancy is the whole point, and on a boat there is no shore to run to. This is the fleet's one hard buying rule, argued out across a season of chef-group threads: pick machines a deckhand could swap a fuse on, carry a spare for the one unit that cannot fail, and never let a single box own two jobs. It is not about performance. It is about what is left standing when something breaks — and at sea, something always does. The showroom sells features; the sea tests survival. When the two disagree, and they will, the field verdict wins. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: A combined unit — washer-dryer, or oven-and-cooktop in one — takes both jobs down when it fails, so buy two separate machines and the second is your built-in backup when one dies mid-charter.
posts.brief: Black-and-white photograph, three-quarter view: two separate stainless galley units standing side by side in tight cabinetry, a strip of gaffer tape labelling each — a visual argument for redundancy. Hard directional light, a deep shadow in the gap between them; matte brushed steel, no branding legible. Forced grayscale on the white card.
posts.why: The fleet's single hardest buying rule lands as one arresting card that reframes a space-saving instinct as a single point of failure. All claims verified against the article; kept unchanged.

posts.hook: The oven is killing your hob
posts.subhook: it isn't broken — it is overheating from below
posts.caption: An induction hob that quits mid-service is almost never broken. Seal it into a worktop directly above an un-ventilated oven, run both flat out through a busy service, and the hob soaks up the oven's heat from below, hits its own thermal limit, and switches itself off — every time the galley gets busy, which is exactly when you cannot afford it. The appliance is doing its job. The install is the fault. Induction has to shed heat downward, and trapping it over a hot box with no airflow guarantees the cutout. The fleet's field fixes run in ascending order of desperation: perforated risers to lift the unit and let air move; vent holes drilled through the surrounding joinery; and, when nothing else works, an engineer's computer fan wedged in beside it to blow the heat away. The deeper cause runs under all three — galleys designed by people who have never cooked a service in one. Before you condemn a hob or call an engineer, check what is sitting beneath it. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: An induction hob that cuts out mid-service usually isn't faulty — trapped in a worktop above an un-ventilated oven, it soaks up the oven's heat, hits its own thermal limit and shuts down to protect itself, so fix the airflow beneath it, not the appliance.
posts.brief: Carousel of grayscale technical-pencil plates, dark #131312 line on off-white, forced grayscale. Frame 1: side-section of a galley worktop — induction hob on top, un-ventilated oven directly beneath, dashed arrows showing the oven's heat trapped against the hob's underside, a small CUT-OUT flag on the hob. Frames 2 to 4: the three field fixes drawn in order — a perforated riser lifting the hob; vent holes bored through the joinery; an engineer's computer fan wedged alongside. Consistent draughtsman labels, no colour.
posts.why: The three field fixes run in a genuine ascending sequence, so the myth-bust reveal plus ordered fixes earn a carousel. All claims verified against the article; kept unchanged.

posts.hook: A second spin risks the motor
posts.subhook: the Creami is a budget Paco — one pass only
posts.caption: A second spin is where the Ninja Creami dies. It earns a galley place as a cheaper Pacojet stand-in — filed by the fleet, affectionately, as a temu Paco — on one strict condition: you spin it once and do not push your luck. It is a budget motor doing a single pass, not a Pacojet built for repeat cycles. Run it back to smooth a stubborn purée and you are gambling with the motor at the worst possible time — mid-charter, with no shore to source a replacement. Treat it as exactly what it is: one machine, one pass, one job. This sits inside a harder equipment truth the fleet argues out — even the Pacojet 4, the reference machine, has drawn field failure reports the maker is said to pin on user error, so nothing here is above scrutiny. Know the limit of every stand-in before you lean on it. Spin once, freeze what you need, and do not run it back. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: The Ninja Creami works as a budget Pacojet stand-in only for a single spin — run it back for a second pass and you gamble with the motor mid-charter.
posts.brief: Black-and-white photograph: a frozen purée in a Creami pint tub, one clean quenelle lifted with a warmed spoon, frost blooming up the container wall. Single hard side light, matte surfaces, no branding legible; the whole frame reads as one clean pass and no more. Forced grayscale on the white card.
posts.why: A one-line operating limit on a tool half the fleet already owns — one card, immediately usable. FIXED: original hook 'A second spin cooks the motor' and fact 'you risk burning out its motor' invented a specific failure mechanism the article does not state; both re-aligned to the article's exact framing ('gamble with the motor'). Caption already used the article's hedged wording, so it stands.

posts.subhook: the richer the plate, the harder it lands
posts.caption:
Fat slows gastric emptying. That is the same lever a GLP-1 drug pulls — Ozempic, Wegovy, Mounjaro all keep food sitting in the stomach far longer, so the guest feels full fast and stays full. Load that plate with butter and cream and you are adding a second pull on the same lever. The rich course you built to tempt an appetite is the one most likely to sit heavily and turn to nausea and reflux.

So when a guest boards on a GLP-1 drug, the instinct to cook richer is exactly backwards. Ease off the confit, the cream sauces, the deep-fried. Lean into grilled white fish, poached chicken, prawns, vegetables, broths, lighter sauces. Plate tasting-size, not charter-size, and serve several small courses rather than one big sitting — light plates sit better than a single heavy one. Keep dessert light: fruit or a sorbet over a rich plated finish.

This is not a lesser menu. It is a more precise one, cooked for the appetite in front of you. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Fat itself slows gastric emptying — the same mechanism a GLP-1 drug uses — so a butter- or cream-heavy course compounds the drug's own effect and is more likely to sit heavily and trigger nausea and reflux.
posts.brief: A single tasting-size grilled white fish fillet on a bare white plate, shot directly from above under hard side light — clean plate rim, no sauce pooling, technical stillness. Greyscale, restrained.
posts.why: One mechanism, one contrarian turn — richness is the wrong move — that lands in a single card and reframes a chef's default instinct.

posts.subhook: and it was never about your cooking
posts.caption:
The plate comes back untouched and it is not a verdict on your cooking. A guest on a GLP-1 drug — Ozempic, Wegovy, Mounjaro — has had their appetite turned down at the source. The drug slows the stomach and quiets the constant food noise in the brain, so someone can eat two forkfuls and be genuinely, physically full. Not polite. Not difficult. Full.

Read it as the drug, not the dish, and the whole service changes. You stop cooking to win them back with something richer — which only sits heavier — and start adjusting down. Take the next course smaller and lighter. Carry on as if the smaller plate were the plan all along, because now it is.

This is the guest quietly rewriting charter cooking. For decades the flex was abundance: more courses, more richness, the groaning table. For this guest the flex is the opposite — precision, restraint, and reading the room honestly. Cook for the appetite in front of you, not the menu you wrote. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: A guest on a GLP-1 drug who stops after two forkfuls is genuinely, physically full — the untouched plate is the drug turning appetite down at the source, not a verdict on your cooking.
posts.brief: A wide charter plate returned to the pass, most of the food untouched with two fork marks left in it, shot top-down under flat window light with the galley pass edge just in frame. Greyscale, quiet, a touch melancholy.
posts.why: The human reframe — it is the drug, not the dish — is one closeable, confessional idea that reaches any professional cook, not only yacht crews.

atlantic-crossing-galley.title: Cooking the Atlantic Crossing: The Galley Playbook

posts.hook: Flat slab, never a deep brick
posts.subhook: A 30 mm slab thaws far faster and stacks to reclaim freezer space.
posts.caption: Freeze the portion flat as a 30 mm slab, not a deep brick, and it thaws in a fraction of the time — and the flat parcels stack, winning back the freezer space a crossing always runs short of. This is the heart of the passage plan, done on dry land two weeks out: kilos of browned mince, lasagne baked in the service dish, curries, braises, soups and vegetable purees. Cook once, divide, label, freeze flat. Chilling to -18C halts microbial growth outright and slows the oxidative staling that would spoil food across three weeks at sea, and the braises actually improve as the flavours marry. Write the dish, the cover count and the date on every parcel in marker, because by week three everything frozen looks identical. Then reheat to a clean core of 75C (165F), every time. In a swell you are not browning mince at a heeling stove — you pull a labelled slab and slide it in the oven. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Freezing a portion flat as a 30 mm slab, rather than in a deep tub, thaws it in a fraction of the time and lets the parcels stack to reclaim freezer space.
posts.brief: Technical-pencil plates, fine graphite on warm white, grayscale. Slide 1: a thin 30 mm slab in a flat freezer bag beside a deep tub brick, a caliper marking the slab's depth. Slides 2-4: a clean make-ahead grid drawn as labelled parcels — browned mince (3 mo), lasagne (2 mo), curries and braises (3 mo), soups and purees (3 mo), pies and meatloaf (2 mo), marinated proteins (2 mo). Marker-scrawled dish, cover count and date on each.
posts.why: Saveable make-ahead reference with a concrete mechanic (30 mm slab) plus verified hold times — the grid is a screenshot chefs keep; earns saves and profile taps.

posts.hook: The unwashed egg keeps for weeks
posts.subhook: Buy ambient; free the cold for what needs it.
posts.caption: An unwashed egg held at cool room temperature keeps for weeks, and many vegetables bought never chilled outlast their fridge-cold cousins by a wide margin. Buy them ambient from the start and you free up cold space for the things that genuinely need it — the fresh salads and herbs you want to eat across days one to five. On a three-week Atlantic passage with no reprovisioning, capacity is the whole menu, so you fight for every litre of cold. Walk the order in tiers: shelf-stable depth first — tinned tomatoes, beans, rice and pasta by the sack, UHT and powdered milk — then the long-keeping veg like potatoes, carrots, roots and squash that hold for weeks in the dark, then the chilled and frozen last, so they spend the least time off the boat. Label every case with its contents and a stow location before it leaves the dock, because at sea you will not be reading handwriting by torchlight in a pitching store. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: An unwashed egg held at cool room temperature keeps for weeks, and many never-chilled vegetables outlast their fridge-cold equivalents, so buying ambient frees cold space for what truly needs it.
posts.brief: Black-and-white photograph: a wooden crate of unwashed eggs and long-keeping roots — potatoes, carrots, squash — in a dim, unrefrigerated dry store, soft directional light raking across the shells. No fridge in frame. Grayscale.
posts.why: A counterintuitive sourcing fact verified in the article that stops mid-scroll and earns a save from anyone who assumes eggs must be refrigerated; distinct lesson from the freezer post.

cook-the-port.title: Cook the Port: Terroir as a Galley Workflow

posts.subhook: The market day is the day after the delivery.
posts.caption: An ingredient at its peak on a Tuesday is a tired version of itself by Friday, so the day you buy matters as much as what you buy. Most harbours run on a delivery rhythm you can learn in one conversation. An island that takes its provisioning on Mondays and Wednesdays has its real market days on Tuesday and Thursday — the day after a delivery, before the good stuff is picked over. Buy then, and you are working with produce at its shortest distance from the boat that landed it. The move that follows: buy the menu, not a list. Walk the stall on the right day, read what is actually best in front of you, and write the card backwards from it. The plan does not arrive with the boat — it gets written at the market. An agent's order is correct and identical in every port; a chef's tastes of the day and the place. Build the walk into the day the way you build in the provisioning run itself, and the rhythm stops being guesswork. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Buy the day after a harbour's provisioning delivery — if the island restocks on Mondays and Wednesdays, the real market days are Tuesday and Thursday, when the stall is freshly stocked and not yet picked over.
posts.brief: Technical-pencil plate: a market crate of just-landed produce on a stall edge, with a small chalk day-sheet propped beside it and one day circled. High-contrast graphite on off-white, grayscale, the circled day the only focal point in frame.
posts.why: A timing tactic most crews get wrong lands as a save-worthy planning rule read between services; every figure (Mon/Wed restock, Tue/Thu market) is verbatim from the article.

posts.subhook: Monaco tomatoes, €30 a kilo. The border, a fraction.
posts.caption: In Monaco, cherry tomatoes can run around €30/kg and a medium sea bass €60–70. Take the short train across the border to Ventimiglia and, in the chef-group's telling, you pay 'a twentieth' of that — for produce just landed and visibly better. That figure is one group's word, not a measured average, but the direction is not in doubt: importing luxury is the expensive habit, and buying what the coast already does best is the cheaper one. The pattern repeats. In St Barths a famous shop charges roughly €1,200 for 125g of caviar that is reasonable a short ferry away. The harbour-luxury markup is real, and it is optional. The catch: the cheaper, better box is almost never the one delivered to the passerelle. It is the one a short train or a back-street stall away, and the arithmetic only works if you do the walking. Build that time into the day the way you build in the provisioning run. Cheaper and better stop being a trade-off the moment you stop paying for the postcode. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: In one chef-group's telling, luxury bought at a harbour markup — Monaco cherry tomatoes near €30/kg — costs about a twentieth a short train away in Ventimiglia, for produce just landed and better.
posts.brief: B&W photo, top-down: two identical crates of cherry tomatoes side by side, one wearing a printed shop label reading €30/kg, the other a plain market crate with a fraction scrawled in chalk. Hard shadow in the gap between them, grayscale, the two price tags the only text in frame.
posts.why: A concrete price shock — €30/kg versus a twentieth — is the most screenshot-worthy fact in the piece, and the post correctly inherits the article's 'in the group's telling' hedge rather than stating it as measured fact.

posts.subhook: Nobody off a yacht does.
posts.caption: Nobody off a yacht usually asks the stall for the thing the town is actually proud of — the lemon, the tomato, the cheese it grows better than anywhere. Ask for it, and the trader lights up. You get the better box, the price the locals pay, and a recommendation you would never have found alone: the catch kept in the freezer behind the right shop, the fish flown in once a week, the day the good stuff lands. None of that is on Google. This is the difference between the agent's list and the chef's. An agent fills an order and it arrives correct, the same in every port; a chef walks the market and lets the place decide the menu. The signal you have got it right is the trader's face. Ask for the headline ingredient, go back a second time, and you stop being another delivery address and become a regular. The intelligence that makes a plate taste of exactly where you are anchored is hyper-local — earned at the stall, by asking and by returning. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Ask a market trader for the ingredient the town is genuinely proud of and you get the better box, the price the locals pay, and hyper-local intel that never reaches Google.
posts.brief: B&W reportage photo: a trader's hands passing a crate of lemons across a stall to a chef's hands, both caught mid-gesture, the trader's face lit. Natural side light, visible grain, grayscale — the exchange between the two people, not the produce, is the subject.
posts.why: The warm, human market move gives the series its nurture-not-flex signature; the core tactic (ask for the headline ingredient to unlock local price and freezer-behind-the-shop intel) is a distinct, article-anchored lesson, not a repeat of the timing or price posts.

sourdough-at-sea.title: Sourdough at Sea: The Science-First Loaf

posts.subhook: The acid that sours the loaf is the acid that keeps it.
posts.caption: A sourdough crumb sits at about pH 4 — and that acidity is not just the tang, it is the shelf life. As the wild culture ferments, its bacteria's lactic and acetic acids acidify the dough enough to hold off the mould and staling that take a plain yeasted loaf in a day or two. So the sourness you taste and the days the loaf keeps are the same chemistry, which is exactly why this is the bread to bake when the next shop is a week off. Store it right and it outlasts anything from the fridge: cut-face down on a board, wrapped in cloth, 3 to 4 days at room temperature. Never the fridge itself — cold air stales bread fastest, drying the crumb while the crust goes leathery. Day three, when the crust has hardened, the loaf earns a second life: griddled for panzanella, blitzed to crumbs, cubed into a ribollita that hides a week of leftovers. For more keeping and more flavour at once, proof it colder and longer for more acid. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: A sourdough's lactic and acetic acids drop the crumb to about pH 4 — acidic enough to hold off the mould and staling that take a yeasted loaf in a day or two — so the tang and the keeping quality are the same chemistry, and a wrapped loaf holds 3 to 4 days at room temperature while the fridge stales bread fastest.
posts.brief: Overhead, grayscale: a sourdough loaf halved on a scrubbed wooden board, cut faces up to show the open blistered crumb, a third piece wrapped in coarse linen alongside. Side light rakes across the crust so the mahogany-dark shell reads as deep charcoal and the crumb holes throw shadow. No fridge, no clutter — the board and cloth say room-temperature keeping.
posts.why: The pH number reframes sour as a provisioning tool — a stop for any chef who has watched bread die before the next shop.

posts.hook: Fire the loaf straight from cold
posts.subhook: The cold proof scores clean, springs high, and waits on your schedule.
posts.caption: A shaped sourdough will hold 12 to 36 hours at 4°C (39°F), then go into a screaming-hot pot straight from the fridge — no warm-up, no second proof. The cold rest uncouples the bake from the proof: mix on a provisioning day, and you fire one or two loaves to order across a whole charter leg instead of mixing fresh dough every dawn. Two things make the cold loaf better, not just convenient. The wild yeast all but crawls at 4°C while the lactic bacteria keep laying down acid, so a longer rest means deeper flavour and a more blistered crust. And a firm, cold dough turns out of the banneton clean and takes one confident scoring slash without dragging or deflating. Preheat a Dutch oven to 250°C (480°F), turn the cold loaf in, lid on 20 minutes, then lid off at 230°C (446°F) to a deep mahogany crust. Judge the bulk ferment by feel — a 40% domed rise — but let the fridge, not the clock, run the finish. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: A shaped sourdough holds 12 to 36 hours in the fridge at 4°C (39°F) and bakes straight from cold with no warm-up, because the long cold proof uncouples the bake from the proof — letting a sole chef fire fresh loaves to order across a whole charter.
posts.brief: A cold, firm boule mid-turn out of a rice-floured banneton onto a semolina-dusted peel, the spiral flour rings crisp on its skin, a single fresh scoring slash opening across the top. Grayscale, hard side light; a cast-iron pot lid just visible steaming at the frame edge to signal the straight-from-cold bake.
posts.why: 'Fire from cold' turns a home-baking rule on its head with a service word chefs live by — the scroll-stop is the contradiction.

posts.hook: The crumb cooks as it cools
posts.subhook: Bake to 96°C, then wait two hours before the first cut.
posts.caption: A sourdough loaf is not finished when the crust looks right — the crumb only truly sets near 96°C (205°F) internal, and it keeps setting as the loaf cools. Pull it on colour alone and the centre bakes gummy; cut it hot and it gums to the knife. Two fixes, both about patience. First, bake to temperature, not appearance: a deep mahogany crust plus a probe reading of 96 to 99°C (205 to 210°F) at the centre. A pale loaf pulled early stays sticky no matter how good the shell looks. Second, cool it on a rack at least 2 hours before the first cut — off the heat the crumb is still cooking, its structure still firming. That wait is the hardest step on a hungry boat, and the one that separates a clean, open slice from a doughy smear. A gummy, sticky centre is almost always doneness, not the culture. Probe it, then walk away. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: A sourdough crumb only truly sets near 96°C (205°F) internal and keeps setting as the loaf cools, so pulling it on crust colour bakes the centre gummy and cutting it hot gums it to the knife — bake to 96 to 99°C, then cool at least 2 hours.
posts.brief: A sourdough halved on a wire cooling rack, a probe thermometer sunk into the centre of the cut face with its dial legible. Grayscale, faint steam-sheen on the open crumb, the dark crust framing bright irregular holes. The rack and the probe say the same thing — bake to temperature, then wait.
posts.why: 'Cooks as it cools' names the carryover every baker rushes past; the gummy-crumb fix pays off in a single read.

sea-urchin-the-candy-of-the-sea.title: Sea Urchin: The Candy of the Sea

posts.hook: Firmness can be faked with alum
posts.subhook: The one tell alum cannot fake is the smell.
posts.caption: Trade uni is often dipped in alum — potassium aluminium sulphate — to stiffen tongues that would otherwise sag. The aluminium ions cross-link the surface proteins, so a lobe that should be slumping stands up firm on the tray. Which means the buying rule most chefs trust — firm equals fresh — can be bought in a bath. A suspiciously rigid, slightly bitter, chemically-clean specimen may be old roe propped up by metal. The tell alum cannot fake is the smell. Uni has no skeleton holding the lobe together; once the animal dies its own enzymes digest the proteins that give the tongue its shape, while cold marine bacteria break the amino acids down into ammonia. Alum stiffens the surface. It does nothing to that reek. A clean, sweet, tide-pool smell is the pass; sharp ammonia or sulphur is walk-away, however firm it looks. Buy ensui — seawater-packed — and mutenka, additive-free roe when you can, hold it at 0–2°C (32–36°F) over ice, never in fresh water, and lead with your nose before your eyes. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Firmness in uni can be faked with alum (potassium aluminium sulphate), which stiffens sagging tongues to disguise age but cannot mask the ammonia of spoilage, so the nose beats the eye.
posts.brief: Overhead B&W macro of two sea-urchin tongues on a slate tray — one crisp and holding its granular curve, the next slumping into liquid; a split-frame firm-versus-melting comparison. Grayscale forces the eye onto structure rather than colour, with a fine pencil annotation line pointing to the collapsing edge.
posts.why: One myth (firm equals fresh) busted with one mechanism — a sourcing rule a working chef can use at the next provisioning stop.

posts.hook: The candy is two amino acids
posts.subhook: Glycine and alanine, not a grain of sugar.
posts.caption: Uni tastes frankly sweet, and there is not a molecule of sugar in it. The sweetness is two free amino acids — glycine and alanine — sitting loose in the tissue and landing straight on your taste receptors. Glycine alone makes up 30–60% of the free-amino-acid pool in green urchin, and it reads as candy on the tongue. The proof is old and clean. In 1964 Komata ran an omission test: strip the roe's amino acids out one at a time and taste what goes missing. Glutamate carried the umami, methionine the unmistakable uni character, and glycine with alanine carried the sweetness. Remove that pair and the candy is gone. This is why the sweetness is a window, not a fixture. The gonad loads with sweet amino acids to feed a spawn, then empties and turns bitter — glutamate collapsing, arginine surging in one study from around 205 to over 700 mg/100g — the moment it releases. You are buying the animal at its single richest hour, so serve it raw, before heat or time inverts the balance. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Uni's candy sweetness contains no sugar — it comes from two free amino acids, glycine (30–60% of the free-amino-acid pool in green urchin) and alanine, isolated by Komata's 1964 taste-omission test.
posts.brief: B&W macro of a single uni tongue split to show its granular surface, lit like a cut gemstone on dark ground; or a technical-pencil plate of a five-lobed urchin cross-section with glycine and alanine labelled in fine hand script. Grayscale pushes the texture and structure forward.
posts.why: One mechanism — sugar-free sweetness named to the molecule — reframes an ingredient chefs think they already understand.

elbulli-foundation-frontier.title: elBulli: The Coastline That Rewrote Cooking

posts.hook: The sphere you prep at anchor
posts.subhook: reverse spherification holds a liquid core for hours
posts.caption:
Reverse spherification puts the calcium inside the liquid — usually calcium lactate gluconate, chosen because, unlike calcium chloride, it doesn't turn bitter — and drops that into a bath of sodium alginate. The gel forms on the outside and then stops. So the sphere holds a liquid core for hours, not the few seconds you get the other way round.

That single difference is why this one belongs at anchor. Basic spherification runs the calcium in the bath, so the gel keeps creeping inward until the centre sets solid — a last-second trick you cannot hold. Reverse pearls you prep hours ahead at anchorage, then plate calm across a rolling pass.

It also carries what basic cannot: dairy, cheese, a splash of cocktail — anything calcium-rich or alcoholic that breaks the classic method. The mechanism is the egg-box: each calcium ion carries two charges and clips between two alginate chains at once, locking them like eggs in a carton. The same reaction, scaled up, is how popping boba is made by the tub.

The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Reverse spherification keeps the calcium inside the liquid so the gel sets only on the outside and then stops, which lets the sphere hold a liquid core for hours — the reason it, not basic spherification, can be prepped ahead at anchor and used with dairy or alcohol.
posts.brief: Overhead B&W photograph: a matte-black slotted spoon lifting one clear liquid-cored sphere from a shallow alginate bath, concentric ripples frozen mid-motion, a small bowl of pale pearls waiting at the frame edge. High tonal range, the single pearl the one luminous point, everything else deep grey.
